mechanism: Lower potential GDP growth reduces equilibrium investment demand and saving-investment balance, depressing the neutral real interest rate
regime_dependence: Holds across both NK (saving/investment) and Post-Keynesian (credit demand shortfall) frameworks; may be partially offset by fiscal expansion

mechanism: Shrinking working-age population directly worsens the dependency ratio, increasing entitlement spending relative to tax base
regime_dependence: Near-mechanical under existing entitlement structures; policy reform could alter trajectory but politically difficult

mechanism: Immigration adds working-age population, directly offsetting the labor input channel of demographic GDP drag by 0.3–0.5pp
regime_dependence: Depends on immigration policy regime; restrictionist trends reduce the offset

mechanism: Demographic labor scarcity drives 3–5% annual labor cost inflation, compounding the refinancing cost squeeze for leveraged borrowers facing the maturity wall
regime_dependence: Strongest in labor-intensive sectors (healthcare, services); timing-dependent on recession coinciding with maturity wall

mechanism: If demographics produce supply-side inflationary shocks (labor scarcity), the dominant risk shifts from demand-side to supply-side, flipping the stock-bond correlation from negative to positive
regime_dependence: Holds when labor scarcity manifests as persistent wage-push inflation rather than demand deficiency

mechanism: Demographic-driven fiscal costs create persistent government bond supply pressure, driving term premium higher as markets demand compensation for fiscal risk
regime_dependence: Holds when fiscal costs are market-funded (Italy path); weakens if costs are monetized (Japan path)

mechanism: Pension/insurance demand compresses credit spreads while demographics simultaneously erode credit quality, creating a divergence between market pricing and fundamental risk
regime_dependence: Divergence accumulates in benign periods; resolves violently in credit downturns

mechanism: Immigration restriction directly reduces credit creation through lower housing formation and mortgage origination ($90–120B annually)
regime_dependence: Strongest in economies with deep mortgage markets and high homeownership rates

mechanism: China's accelerating demographic decline widens growth and rate differentials vs. demographically healthier economies, creating FX and equity relative value opportunities
regime_dependence: Modulated by policy responses (stimulus, capital controls, currency management)

mechanism: Lower r* environment improves pension funded status, accelerating derisking and concentrating demand at 20y–30y maturities, leaving the 7y–10y belly cheap
regime_dependence: Strongest when funded status is near or above 100%; weakens if rates rise sharply and funded status overshoots

mechanism: Lower potential GDP growth reduces corporate revenue growth, creating a 1–2% annualized drag on equity returns, amplified at higher starting valuations
regime_dependence: Drag is more severe at higher starting P/E multiples; partially offset by margin expansion and buybacks

mechanism: Immigration-driven credit creation accelerates the Minsky hedge→speculative→Ponzi transition during demographic booms; absence of immigration extends balance sheet recessions
regime_dependence: Strongest in financialized economies with deep credit markets

mechanism: Aging HNW investors in semi-liquid credit vehicles will eventually draw down, potentially triggering gate-contagion that reverses the demographic bid and reprices credit spreads
regime_dependence: Triggered by macro stress coinciding with demographic cohort drawdown; untested scenario
